data.title: Marti: A Potential Early-Stage Opportunity Has Emerged
data.description:
I covered Uber (UBER) last week, where I communicated a positive outlook on the e-hailing stock. Today's article discusses a similar name, Marti Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:MRT), which is an emerging market e-hailing company situated in Turkey. Although not as established as Uber, Marti shares similar features while providing concentrated emerging market exposure.

I've decided to add Marti to my watchlist and will likely consider a position when my portfolio's cash position has settled high by enough; here's why I'm bullish.

Marti: An Overview

I think the easiest way to describe the business is by quoting its own statement, which reads as: "Marti is Türkiye’s leading mobility app, offering a wide variety of transportation services. Marti operates a ride-hailing service that matches riders with car, motorcycle and taxi drivers; delivery services; and a large fleet of rental e-mopeds, e-bikes and e-scooters. All of Marti’s offerings are serviced by proprietary software systems and IoT infrastructure."

In essence, the company is an early-stage version of Uber with the add-on of self-use services. In addition, it has emerging market focus (Turkey). Don't forget that Turkey has a population of over 86 million people (this excludes travellers) and that technological transfer (such as e-hailing apps) from developed markets remains in an early-stage (explained later in the article).

Marti operates in 20 of Turkey's largest cities, covering 80% of national GDP and it's the most downloaded urban mobility app in the region, illustrating successful market targeting and capture.

As previously mentioned the company is multi-service. Notably the company has grown its tips and revenue per customer at scale due to core offerings and the synergies provided by motorcycle offerings – as shown by the following figure, 82% of Marti's motorcycle users have used another Marti service.

Key Drivers

Scale and Potential Operating Leverage

A concern with some earlier-stage tech/services firms is monetisation. Many achieve spectacular reach. However, few convert reach into revenue and profitability. Marti has illustrated an ability to monetise with its 2025 revenue increasing by 110.3% year-over-year amid its first year of full platform monetisation. In addition, the company illustrated a degree of operating leverage, whereby its adjusted EBITDA margin improved to -31% from 2024's -104%. Share-based compensation remains high ($11.3 million in 2025), which has influenced non-core EBITDA by a tremendous amount. However, the early signs are that Marti might achieve sustained profitability once it reaches a consolidation phase.

Figure 4 illustrates Marti's 2026 guidance. The company's management thinks revenue will grow to $70 million for the full-year. Moreover, the company expects adjusted EBITDA to turn positive. Are these targets realistic? I think so. Revenue has scaled significantly of late. For example, total rides reached 50.4 million during fiscal 2025, a 60.3% year-on-year increase. In addition, Marti's revenue and EBITDA base might benefit from SG&A stabilisation – SG&A charges decreased by 43% in 2025 alone.

Multi-channel and Dynamic Pricing

The company's multi-channel is really driving growth, echoed by the fact that Marti opened in 16 additional cities during fiscal 2025. In addition, the company launched its delivery platform as an additional integration. Running integrated services allows the firm to analyse its end users' behaviour carefully, which leads to more effective price setting and efficient marketing (the platforms don't have to be marketed independently). The strategy has carried Marti to 3.8M all-time unique ride-hailing riders and 490 thousand all-time registered ride-hailing drivers (recorded in March 2026).

Fleet Ownership and Subscriptions

Marti owns its own fleet of two-wheeled vehicles, including e-bikes, e-mopeds, and e-scooters, currently totalling ~23,200. Certain investors might deem direct ownership inefficient, seeing as vehicles are depreciating assets. However, I consider the fleet ownership an advantage for two reasons.

1) It might be difficult to operate e-hailing with e-bikes, e-mopeds, and e-scooters and 3rd party sourcing could be ineffective.

2) Large cities in Turkey are ideal for e-bikes, e-mopeds, and e-scooters from a landscape point of view and the offering differentiates Marti from companies such as Uber in that regard.

3) Direct ownership has allowed Marti to run a subscription-based model, where the drivers pre-pay rides, which smooths out Marti's revenue and maintain end market engagement.

Multiples and Stock Pricing

Marti is still in an early-stage growth phase. As such, I think it's fair to judge it based on sales multiples until it exits its early-stage growth phase.

Marti operates in a unique geographic market, making a comparison to Uber or Lyft (LYFT) unreliable. Instead, I think we should look at the trajectory of key multiples while supplementing with growth metrics. For example, I looked at the company's price-to-sales and enterprise value/sales multiples (figure 6). Both metrics are significantly lower on a forward basis, which is unsurprising, given that Marti is in early-stage full monetisation. I think the evolution of these two metrics is exceptionally encouraging. I would however monitor them closely and start tracking metrics like EV/EBITDA and price-to-cash flow when market reaches its consolidation phase.

Metric Value
Price/Sales TTM 5.67
Price Sales Fwd 2.31
EV/Sales TTM 6.12
EV/Sales Fwd 3.44

Source: Seeking Alpha (Figure 6)

I don't believe in technical analysis as a standalone technique. However, I think we can infer a lot by looking at Marti's moving averages. The stock has lost more than 30% of its value year-on-year, dragging it below its 100- and 200-day moving averages. However, Marti recently eclipsed its 10 and 50-day moving averages during a post U.S.-Iran war recovery and a positive earnings report (April 13th), suggesting a reversal might be underway.

Balance Sheet Analysis

I wanted to isolate Marti's balance sheet in a discreet section to highlight a few factors, Figure 8 illustrates the business' consolidated balance sheet; a discussion follows.

Here are a few matters worth considering:

As shown in the balance sheet, Marti has a current ratio of 0.97, suggesting thin liquidity. Marti is an early-stage growth company. I wouldn't say its current ratio is particularly odd for an early-stage growth company. However, liquidity can be difficult to ascertain if investors' sentiment turns risk-off. Furthermore, Marti has issued a substantial amount of convertible notes financed at double-figure interest rates, which could lead to payment-in-kind (additional debt issuance) or default, if the company doesn't fulfil its obligations.

Is this all bad news for Marti? No, not really, it just reflects on the risks owned by early-stage investors. I think the company's growth is staggering and its profitability outlook is encouraging. Marti will likely retain confidence from both equity and debt investors. In addition, Marti might receive an option to refinance if it turns EBITDA positive (by issuing new, cheaper debt). That being said, as highlighted before, market circumstances can dictate investors' risk appetite. Moreover, a risk remains that Marti could fail to reach its operating and financial targets, which would in-turn lower investor confidence. Although I doubt the latter would occur, one can never be too sure.

Risks

Historical Stock Performance

Marti's historical returns haven't been great. Why exactly? Marti slumped in 2023 when it was announced that it would enter a SPAC deal, the company has yet to achieve profitability, and full monetisation only occurred in fiscal 2025. Although I don't see these risks repeating themselves, Marti's left tail risk cannot be ignored.

Competitive Landscape

While Marti has secured early-stage market share, it should be considered that it will face competition if it chooses to expand beyond its current market, especially if it enters e-hailing territories dominated by the likes of Uber and Lyft. Marti can work around the competition by focusing on self-owned two-wheel vehicles; however, expansion would be expensive and capital intensive.

FX Translation

Marti reports in USD. However, its local currency is the Turkish Lira, which is an exceptionally volatile currency. The Lira won't influence Marti's economic results as the company's functional currency and local currency are the same. However, we could easily see the Lira's volatility filter into reporting amounts, which would influence Marti's American Depositary Receipts.

Final Word

I've decided to add Marti Technologies, Inc. to my watchlist. Despite its high risk profile, I'm bullish about the stock's prospects given its hyper-growth, emerging market access, and single market focus. Moreover, I think the company might achieve profitability in 2026 or 2027, which could result in significant traction from investors.

Marti is undoubtedly a risky stock. However, I'm eying a small stake as I think the stock's upside could be immense and that downside risk has mostly been priced-in.

data.title: United Airlines And American Airlines Merger Looks Huge But It Shouldn't Happen
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) has floated the idea to acquire American Airlines (AAL). Such a combination would be the biggest move in airline consolidation since the consolidation round in 2001. In this report, I discuss what a combined airline would look like and where we see major hurdles for such a combination.

American Airlines And United Airlines Are Compatible But With Major Mismatches

If we look at the airline fleets, we see that the single aisle fleet is highly compatible, which does not come as a surprise as the fleet is built around the Airbus A320 and Boeing 737 families, which brings operational synergies through maintenance, schedule flexibility and pilot common type ratings. In the large single aisle segment, we note a subtle difference with American Airlines having a high A321 concentration whereas United Airlines is more focused on the Boeing 737-900/Boeing 737 MAX 9 and Boeing 757. American Airlines phased out its last Boeing 757 in 2020. So, there is a high compatibility with a partial legacy fleet mismatch.

In the wide body category, American Airlines has a more standardized fleet built around the Boeing 777 and Boeing 787. United Airlines operates the Boeing 767, Boeing 777 and Boeing 787. Similar to the Boeing 757, American Airlines phased out its last 767s in 2020. So, in the wide body segment we see moderate compatibility, but with a good baseline for wide body integration.

The image above shows the combined network of United Airlines and American Airlines. Just by looking at the map, there is not a lot that can be said, but when viewing the individual maps, unsurprisingly there is a major overlap on the route network.

A view of the hubs shows that American Airlines and United Airlines are seemingly extremely complementary with both airlines having their own set of hubs with overlap in Los Angeles and Chicago. The entire merger ball started rolling with US Transportation Secretary Duffy stating he was open to mergers between the larger airlines, but they would need to shed assets:

If there was a merger between some of the larger airlines, they would have to peel off ​some of their assets.

So, more than likely in a combination Chicago, Los Angeles and potentially the New York area is where both airlines would need to adjust. It would require a combination of slot divestitures, hub downsizing and route eliminations. For American and United, the hub combination looks extremely strong, but the relatively good complementary structure of the hubs is because most airlines try to create hubs where they do not directly compete.

Data from the Bureau of Transportation Logistics shows that in terms of market share American Airlines is ranked #2 and United Airlines is ranked #4. If we combine American’s 17.4% market share with United’s 16.7% market share, we get to a market share of 34.1% which would create a combined airline almost twice the size of Delta Air Lines.

There are two mismatches between American Airlines and United Airlines. The first one is that American Airlines is part of the Oneworld alliance, while United Airlines is part of Star Alliance. These global airline alliances provide strength to international operations and if American Airlines and United Airlines were to merge, they would either have to choose to become part of one of the alliances or become a player without affiliation to either of the alliances.

If we compare both airlines, we see that United Airlines margins are substantially better with EBITDA margins being 13% compared to 7% for American Airlines. Free cash flow margins are 4% for United and -1% for American Airlines. American Airlines sports a massive $30.4 billion net debt bringing its leverage to 7.8x compared to United Airlines’ debt of $18.8 billion with a leverage of 2.4x. American Airlines now trades at 9.8x EV/EBITDA close to their median valuation level while United Airlines is undervalued trading at 6.4x EV/EBITDA compared to a median of 7.5x. At the start of the year, airline names traded at 7.6x EV/EBITDA, so American Airlines is not cheap. It trades more in line with the profitable airlines group.

American Airlines And United Airlines Is A Match That Perhaps Should Not Happen

Generally, I am in favor of market mechanism working. However, if I look at a combination of American Airlines and United Airlines from various angles I do not believe that this is a match that should be pursued. Looking at the financial metrics, there can be synergies once combined but American Airlines brings an extremely high debt load with it. The subpar cash flow and margin performance is likely why United Airlines would be interested in American Airlines in the first place, but it brings a lot of execution risk. Furthermore, acquisition would be funded by debt or shares or a combination of both. So, investors in United Airlines would face the prospect of owning part of a subpar performer with execution risk with higher combined leverage or even having to share disproportionately with existing shareholders of American Airlines.

The fleets and network seem to work reasonably well, but with massive overlap that would require divestures and route capacity adjustments or outward eliminations. Furthermore, we note the mismatch on the alliance which can be fixed by choosing one of the alliances or not be a member of any alliance. However, both of these elements already may make a combination less attractive. The overlap of scale and the scale of both airlines also make this a regulatory nightmare with complex antitrust issues to be resolved.

I do understand that United would be interested in merging with American or acquiring it with the mindset that it can substantially increase the operational performance. However, there is major execution risk and regulatory concerns given the size and overlap of these airlines. With airline operating costs having risen substantially and a potential pressure on demand, this would be a good time to explore M&A. However, I also believe that airlines have substantially increased costs at a time when employees had leverage do demand higher pay and we are not seeing margins squeezed as fuel costs have substantially risen. For airlines, not all things can be solved by simply combining operations and cutting overlap costs. When demand growth and pricing power weakens, airlines also have to adjust their individual cost balances and that has yet to happen.

Conclusion: I Don’t Believe In A UAL-AAL Combination

Overall, the timing of the combination idea floating makes sense but I believe that this is not a combination that is easy to execute given the size of the airlines facing increased scrutiny even when government officials are not rejecting the possibility of large combinations. Furthermore, American Airlines’ major debt load is a huge issue in any business combination.

data.title: PTA: Worry About Inflation, Not The Problems In Private Credit
data.description:
The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund (PTA) is a closed-end fund that income-focused investors may choose to purchase as a means of earning a high level of current income from the assets in their portfolios. The fund performs reasonably well at providing its investors with an income, as it boasts an 8.40% yield at the current share price. This is well above the yields of just about any equity or fixed-income index. However, the fact that this fund invests in fixed-income securities could result in it having lower total returns going forward than equity funds, and this could hinder the fund’s ability to preserve an investor’s wealth in real terms. As there appears to be a chance that the United States may be able to enter a period of stagflation, this could be a very real problem.

About The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund

The website for the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund states that the fund’s primary investment objective is to provide its shareholders with a high level of current income. However, the website does not provide any strategy description, nor does it tell us what kinds of securities the fund will invest in. All it provides is the following graphic:

This does not really tell us too much, as most closed-end funds would likely claim to invest in securities issued by:

“High-quality and historically stable companies with attractive relative values.”

Indeed, pretty much any large-capitalization equity fund could say the same thing. This description does not even state whether the fund invests in bonds, equities, or something else.

Fortunately, we can get a bit of an idea of what the fund’s portfolio might contain by looking at the name of it. The name of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund suggests that the portfolio will primarily consist of preferred stocks and fixed-income securities. However, there are instances where the name of a fund does not accurately describe what is in its portfolio. For example, as I showed in a previous article, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI) actually has a portfolio that is almost evenly split between preferred stocks and bonds, despite the name of the fund strongly suggesting that it would be invested in preferred stocks. As such, we cannot always go by the name of a fund to determine its composition.

The largest positions in PTA’s portfolio are all preferred stocks:

This fits with the name of the fund and suggests that the fund’s assets may, in fact, primarily be invested in preferred stock issues. The fund’s most recent holdings report does confirm that the fund’s assets are primarily invested in preferred securities. This document provides the following asset allocation as of January 31, 2026:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Exchange-Traded Funds – Corporate Bonds

0.2%

Preferred Securities – Exchange-Traded

27.5%

Preferred Securities – Over-the-Counter

121.5%

Corporate Bonds – Real Estate

0.2%

U.S. Treasury Notes

0.5%

Money Market Funds

0.4%

This document states that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund actually includes a mixture of preferred securities and corporate bonds, although it is very heavily weighted towards preferred securities. In addition, we should note that some of the securities that are specifically identified as being corporate bonds are not bonds at all. For example, the fund’s holdings report specifically states that the exchange-traded funds in its portfolio are corporate bond ETFs. This is not the case, as the same document also states that the only exchange-traded fund in PTA’s portfolio is the Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX):

The Invesco Preferred ETF is not a corporate bond ETF. The website for that fund states:

The Invesco Preferred ETF is based on the ICE BofAML Core Plus Fixed Rate Preferred Securities Index.

In other words, PGX is an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the ICE Bank of America Merrill Lynch Core Plus Fixed Rate Preferred Securities Index. That is a preferred stock index, not a corporate bond index. As the strategy for most exchange-traded index funds is simply to purchase all of the securities that are included in their benchmark indices in roughly equal proportions to what the index itself has, PGX invests its assets exclusively in preferred stocks. Thus, that 0.2% allocation to exchange-traded corporate bond ETFs should rightfully be combined with the fund’s preferred stock allocation rather than its corporate bond allocation. This is important due to the simple fact that corporate bonds and preferred stocks do not deliver identical performance in the market.

Thus, approximately 149.2% of the fund’s net assets are invested in preferred stocks, and 0.7% of its assets are invested in bonds (both corporate and fixed income). This assumes, however, that we consider the contingent capital securities in the fund’s portfolio to be preferred stock rather than bonds. Unlike funds such as the Flaherty & Crumrine preferred stock funds, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund does not consider contingent capital securities to be a different type of security than preferred stocks. However, as Investopedia points out, contingent capital securities are actually debt securities, so they are more akin to bonds than to preferred stock. However, as is the case with preferred stocks, contingent capital securities do not usually have maturity dates. In addition, contingent capital securities can be converted into common stock in certain circumstances. This arguably makes them similar to perpetual convertible bonds or convertible preferred securities; however, unlike convertibles, investors in contingent capital securities do not usually want them to convert because they only convert into common equities whenever something is going wrong with the company. Contingent capital securities were created as a way to reduce the risk that a bank will need a government bailout, like what occurred back in 2008. In other words, these securities are intended as a way for investors in the bank to share in the losses should the bank’s capital fall below a certain level.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund’s documentation does not state what proportion of the fund’s assets are invested in continent capital securities as opposed to traditional preferred stock. However, most European banks issued contingent capital securities instead of preferred stock whenever they need to raise capital. The fund’s most recent holdings report provides this country allocation for its portfolio as of January 31, 2026:

This states that 18.8% of the fund’s assets are invested in securities issued by companies in the European Union. Furthermore, the fund had 8.7% of its assets invested in securities issued by entities based in the United Kingdom, along with 4.3% of its assets invested in securities issued by Swiss companies. There is a very strong possibility that at least some of these securities are actually contingent capital securities rather than traditional preferred stock.

One thing that we notice if we look at the fund’s largest positions (see above) is that the majority of its largest positions are in banks or other financial institutions. After all, eight of the ten largest positions are banks, and the other two are both brokerage houses. However, in a certain sense, they are all banks because the two companies that are listed as brokerage houses – Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS) – both have banking charters and even offer a selection of bank accounts. Thus, these two companies could also be considered to be banks, depending on one’s perspective. This may lead potential investors to conclude that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is very heavily invested in the banking sector. This is indeed the case, as 53.68% of the fund’s total assets are invested in banks:

That is obviously more than half of the fund’s assets invested in the banking sector. While this is certainly a lot of exposure to a single sector, we can see that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is actually underweight to the banking sector compared to its benchmark index (which is a blended benchmark that consists of a 40% allocation to the ICE BofA 7% Constrained DRD Eligible Preferred Securities Index, a 35% allocation to the ICE BofA US Investment Grade Institutional Capital Securities Index, and a 25% allocation to the Bloomberg Developed Market USD Contingent Capital Index). It is also not usual for a preferred stock closed-end fund to have a large allocation to the banking sector due simply to the fact that the banking sector is the largest issuer of preferred stocks in the market. We can see this in the simple fact that the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), which tracks the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index, has a 61.71% allocation to financial institutions:

The Invesco Preferred ETF, which is the only exchange-traded fund in PTA’s portfolio, does not disclose what proportion of its portfolio is invested in the banking sector. However, if we look at the largest positions in that ETF, we do see a lot of banks:

As clearly shown, there are only two positions out of the ETF’s top ten holdings that are not banks. Clearly, then, PTA is not alone among preferred stock funds in its high exposure to banks.

Banks And Private Credit Exposure

The fact that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has a sizable allocation to the banking sector could be concerning for some investors, given the troubles that appear to be brewing in the private credit market. After all, some of us can remember back to the 2007 to 2009 period when a surge in defaults on subprime mortgage loans triggered a financial crisis and a recession. Following its peak in October 2007, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) fell 48.43% by the end of March 2009:

(The chart shows the price performance of the S&P 500 Index from October 1, 2007, through March 31, 2009).

At the time, that was one of the largest market declines during any eighteen-month period, rivaling only the market’s performance during the Great Depression. Banking stocks and the stocks of other major financial institutions were hit especially hard, with Citigroup (C) shares losing 94.70% and American International Group (AIG) shares declining a whopping 98.54% over that period:

The preferred stocks of banks were also hit very hard by the financial crisis, with PFF falling 51.59% over the same period. As such, investors certainly have a reason to be concerned if the currently unfolding problems in the private credit market cause a similar crisis to occur.

The current concerns surround private loans made to software companies that could see their businesses adversely disrupted by emerging artificial intelligence technology. Private credit is a somewhat obscure area of finance, as it differs a lot from the stocks, bonds, and bank loans with which most individuals are familiar. In private credit, a non-bank entity makes a loan to a company. These loans may be made to companies that otherwise have difficulty getting a loan from a bank or issuing bonds in the capital markets, or they may be an alternative to bank loans. For example, a company might be able to get a private loan with a lower interest rate or with better terms than a bank would demand. This is basically the segment of the economy that business development companies operate in, but they are not the only entities that make private loans to businesses. There are, for example, private equity funds that make such loans. The important thing to keep in mind is that banks are not the entities that make private loans. In addition, private credit tends to be relatively illiquid, and the lender cannot usually easily or quickly sell the loan to another party.

As of right now, the total amount of outstanding private loans is approximately $1.8 trillion. That is about the same size as the subprime mortgage market was at its 2007 peak. However, the economy as a whole and the total amount of financial wealth today are much larger than was the case back in 2007. Thus, as a proportion of the economy or markets, private credit is significantly smaller than subprime mortgages were. This by itself reduces the possibility that a surge of defaults on private loans will cause another massive financial crisis on the same scale as the subprime mortgage crisis.

The root of the fears surrounding private credit right now comes specifically from the loans that business development companies and private funds made to software companies, which account for approximately 25% of the private loans outstanding. One of the jobs that is expected to be disrupted by generative artificial intelligence is software coding. Some artificial intelligence-powered chatbots already on the market, such as Anthropic’s Claude, are capable of assisting users with writing software code. This could make it much easier for companies that wish to use customized software programs to write the programs themselves instead of contracting with an outside party. From Morningstar:

The concern in both markets is that AI threatens the profit margins and underlying business models of many software companies by reducing the barriers to entry and enabling customers to build their own software. Those worries strike at the heart of the reasons many lenders had for issuing the loans: fat profit margins for software companies, stable customer bases, and reliable revenue from license subscriptions.

If customers are able to build their own software, then there is no need for them to pay a recurring fee to another company that built the software that they are using. These subscription fees are a reliable source of revenue for software developers, and this is the revenue that some of these companies were depending on to cover the payments on the private loans that they took out.

Private credit lenders are already starting to suffer from rising defaults. According to Fitch Ratings, the default rate on private loans was 9.2% in 2025, a 1.1% increase over the 8.1% default rate that was reported in 2024. As we all know, whenever a borrower defaults, the lender has a high risk of losing most or all of the money that they lent out to the borrower. Thus, it is easy to see how a rising default rate can start causing those companies that participate in the private credit market to start to lose money.

However, this situation is different from what the world experienced back over the 2007 to 2009 period. As was already mentioned, private loans are not made by banks. These loans are generally made by business development companies or by private funds that require all of their investors to be accredited (meaning that only wealthy people can invest). The problem that occurred during the subprime mortgage crisis is that several of the largest banks were actually holding the loans in their portfolios. While it is true that securitization of mortgages passes a significant portion of the default risk onto whoever buys the securities, it was not private investors who purchased the subprime mortgage securities, it was some of the largest banks in the world. Once those mortgages started defaulting, the banks began to scramble to get rid of them, but all the buyers had disappeared. As such, the banks could not sell the securities without realizing huge losses, and the defaults meant that they were suffering losses anyway. Private loans are not securitized, and the lenders typically keep them on their own balance sheets, so the individuals losing money to defaults on these loans will be the business development companies and investors who put money into private credit funds rather than the banks.

With that said, it is possible that banks in general may have limited exposure to problems in the private credit market. This comes from the fact that banks have made loans to providers of private credit. For example, a bank might lend money to a business development company that then turns around and lends this money out to a borrower who is seeking money. It is not an atypical strategy for a business development company to do this in order to earn higher yields on its net assets. The risk to the bank comes from the fact that if too many borrowers default, then the private credit provider might not be able to pay back the loan. However, given that most entities that make private loans have relatively diverse portfolios that will continue to generate revenue even if software companies default in increasing numbers, it seems unlikely that all of them will default. In addition, the total amount of loans that banks have provided to private credit providers is only about $300 billion, which is a relatively small proportion of total bank loans outstanding.

Thus, it seems unlikely that the troubles in private lending will result in a financial crisis. It also seems very unlikely that banks will end up missing payments that they have promised to their preferred stockholders as a result of the troubles in private credit. Thus, I doubt that this is anything that investors in the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund need to worry about.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities And Income Fund Versus Indices And Peers

As of the time of writing, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has an 8.40% yield. This is a substantially higher yield than that provided by any of the major broad-market preferred stock or fixed-income indices. We can see that in this comparison chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.93%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.60%

Vanguard Total World Bond Fund (BNDW)

4.17%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

4.46%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.04%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

5.74%

As of right now, the highest-yielding of the major American or global fixed-income indices is the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks the price and yield performance of junk bonds issued by American-domiciled countries. It is, admittedly, not particularly surprising that this is currently the highest-yielding of the major fixed-income indices, as it has had this status for most of the past few years. Furthermore, the yield of this index has been pretty stable in the mid-6% range since October of 2023:

This chart shows the yield of the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF since October 1, 2023. As we can see, the yield of that particular fund has ranged from about 6.50% to 6.80% for nearly the entire period.

Regular readers of my articles will likely notice that the yield of the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF, which tracks the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index, has come down significantly in the past few weeks. For example, in an article that was published in mid-February, I showed that the preferred stock index had a 6.19% yield. This chart shows the yield of the preferred stock index from January 1, 2023, through today:

As we can clearly see, the yield of that particular fund was above 6% for nearly the entire multi-year period. The current yield of preferred stocks is among the lowest that we have seen in many years. This is actually rather surprising given that the Consumer Price Index just posted a massive 0.9% month-over-month increase in March that was also sufficient to cause a 3.3% year-over-year increase in the same index:

As we can see, the most recent figures for the rate of change in the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers were the highest that was seen in over a year. This index claims to measure the rate of inflation as experienced by consumers, so we would think that this would cause an increase in fixed-income yields for two reasons:

  1. The higher the rate of inflation, the lower the returns that investors get from fixed-income securities in real terms. Therefore, investors will usually demand higher yields so that they still end up getting an acceptable real rate of return.
  2. The Federal Reserve usually increases interest rates whenever inflation is higher.

While preferred stocks are normally considered to be equities rather than debt, they are similar to bonds in that all of the net returns that investors earn from them over their lifetimes come from the direct payments that they make to their investors. Thus, we would normally expect preferred stock yields to decline whenever inflation rises. However, that was not the case recently.

Admittedly, there are some analysts and market participants who expect that the apparent inflationary surge in March will be short-lived. After all, the largest contributor to the increase in the Consumer Price Index during that month was the rapid increase in crude oil prices that accompanied the outbreak of the Iran War. Thus, there may be an expectation that crude oil prices will go down once the war ends and reverse the apparent inflationary trend. If investors expect the inflation to be short-lived, then it could explain why preferred stock yields are actually lower today than they were in February. However. As I pointed out in a recent article, there were signs of worsening inflation before the surge in oil prices. In particular, the US Import/Export Price Indices and the February Producer Price Index were both pointing to annualized inflation rates of well over 8% (and that is using the February numbers, which were before the Iran War even started). Should this prove to continue, then inflation may not come down as quickly as the market expects. This makes it very difficult to justify the recent decline in preferred stock yields.

Naturally, it is important to understand the recent performance of preferred stocks, as well as where they could be heading when considering a position in the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund. This is simply because the fund itself is primarily invested in preferred stocks, as we have already discussed.

Other closed-end funds have a much easier time matching the yield of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund have a much easier time matching the yield of the fund for two reasons:

  1. Peer closed-end funds employ leverage as a means of boosting their yields. The index funds do not.
  2. Peer closed-end funds are actively managed and regularly realize capital gains, which can be distributed to the investors in addition to their income. Index funds do not typically do this.

The fact that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund uses leverage is the reason why its preferred stock allocation is more than 100% of its net assets. The fund has borrowed money to purchase preferred stock, which allows it to collect dividend payments from more securities than it could afford with just its net assets, and these additional dividend payments boost the effective yield that the fund earns. Most closed-end preferred stock funds use a similar strategy. Here is how PTA’s yield compares with that of its peers:

Fund Name

The median yield of this group of five funds is the 9.08% current yield of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund. As such, PTA has a yield that is somewhat below the peer median level. This could be something of a turn-off for investors who are seeking to maximize their incomes, since they will obviously get less income with PTA than they would get with three of the peer funds. However, the fund’s lower-than-median yield could also be a sign that the market expects that the fund will be able to sustain its distributions at the current level going forward. However, we should note that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has failed to cover its distributions year-to-date:

This chart shows the fund’s net asset value since the start of the year. As we can see, it has declined by 1.52% over the period, which immediately tells us that the fund has paid out more than it has earned from its investment portfolio since the start of the year. This is not consistent with a sustainable distribution, but as we are only three and a half months into the year, it is still possible that the fund’s performance will improve as the year progresses. This could easily be the case if inflationary pressures ease and interest rates come down. Personally, though, I am somewhat doubtful that this will be the case.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund was on September 3, 2025. As such, just over seven months have passed since we last looked at this fund. We have already discussed many of the things that had an impact on the performance of preferred stocks, and as we have also seen, preferred stock yields have come down somewhat. As we can see here, the shares of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund have fallen 6.00% since the last time that we looked at the fund:

This is a significantly worse performance than the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index delivered over the same period, which is likely to be somewhat disheartening. Another thing that we notice is that the price of the index fund also declined along with its yield. On September 3, 2025, when I published my previous article on this fund, the index fund was yielding 6.52%. One reason for this is that banks frequently issue preferred stocks that pay a yield that is variable based on some underlying interest rate benchmarks. For example, take a look at the largest positions in the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF:

The Citigroup Capital XIII preferred issue specifically states that it pays a yield that varies depending on the movement of the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. The Secured Overnight Financing Rate typically tracks the federal funds rate relatively closely. The federal funds rate fell by 75 basis points since our last discussion. As such, all of the variable-rate securities held in PFF’s portfolio experienced declining dividend payments over the period. This reduced the index fund’s income, which naturally forced it to reduce its distributions. This is how both the price and the yield could decline at the same time.

If we compare the performance of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund to the index on a total return basis, we get this:

This is, unfortunately, still rather unlikely to encourage potential investors. After all, PTA still underperformed the index despite its higher yield. Investors in it also lost money, whereas investors in the index fund did manage to make a positive return (although admittedly, a 1.76% total return over the course of seven months is not especially impressive).

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.1340 per share ($1.608 per share annually). This gives it an 8.40% yield at the current price, which is higher than most index funds but somewhat lower than what other preferred stock-focused closed-end funds are able to deliver.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has maintained its current distribution since the start of 2023. However, this does not actually mean that it can sustain it indefinitely, nor does it mean that the fund is actually earning sufficient profits to cover the current distribution. We should have a look at its financials to see how well it is actually covering the distribution, and as of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for this fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025. While this report is several months old at this point, it is still the newest financial report that is available for this fund, and it is also newer than the financial reports that we had available to us the last time that we discussed this fund. As such, it should work reasonably well to provide us with an update on the fund’s financial situation.

For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund reported a net investment income of $63,544,195, which was not sufficient to cover the $88,879,719 that the fund paid out to its shareholders in the form of distributions over the period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, PTA reported net realized gains of $35,194,728, which were partially offset by net unrealized losses of $9,195,924. Overall, the fund’s portfolio produced net investment profits of $89,542,999 over the twelve-month period:

This was sufficient to cover the distribution, and the fund was able to fully cover its distribution solely with its net investment income and net realized gains. This is the ideal situation for investors.

Valuation

Shares of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund are currently trading at a 7.29% discount on net asset value. This is very much in line with the 7.27% discount that the shares of this fund have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is reasonable compared to its long-term average valuation. Over the past five years, the shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from a 0.76% discount to a 13.97% discount on net asset value. The average price over the trailing five-year period was a 6.57% discount, which is not quite as good as the price that the fund has today.

Here is how the current price of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities Income Fund

(7.29%)

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

(4.67%)

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund

(7.88%)

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

2.05%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

0.13%

The median fund in terms of valuation is FPF, and that fund is more expensive than PTA at its current level. As such, the fund looks reasonably priced relative to its peers in today’s environment. As this fund is also reasonably priced compared to its own historical valuation, the price today looks reasonable for an investor who wants to acquire shares of the fund.

Expenses

The website for the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund states that the fund has an expense ratio of 4.36%. While that certainly seems to be very high, we should note that a substantial proportion of this fund’s expenses is due to the fact that it needs to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude the interest expenses, the fund’s expense ratio is 1.66%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is a closed-end fund that could be an option for an investor who wants to earn a high level of current income from the assets in their portfolio. As of the time of writing, the fund has an 8.40% yield, which compares pretty well to most fixed-income index funds. However, with inflation rising, it is uncertain how well the fund will do in real terms. In February, the Producer Price Index and the US Import/Export Indices both rose at annualized rates that are higher than this fund’s current yield, after all. When combined with the inflationary pressures from the rising oil prices in March, a fixed-income fund like this one might struggle to adequately compensate its investors.

data.title: Mar Vista U.S. Quality Q1 2026 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Mar Vista U.S. Quality Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary.

Mar Vista's U.S. Quality strategy returned -7.24% net-of-fees in the first quarter of 2026. The Russell 1000 Index and the S&P 500 Index returned -4.18% and -4.33%, respectively. Stock selection within healthcare and materials positively impacted performance, while stock selection in industrials and information technology detracted the most from performance during the quarter.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new investments in Ecolab Inc. (ECL), GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) and QXO Incorporated (QXO). We also exited our positions in Salesforce Inc. (CRM), SAP ADR (SAP) and Veralto (VLTO). We added to our holdings in Broadcom (AVGO), GE Aerospace (GE), Netflix (NFLX) and NVIDIA (NVDA) and trimmed Intuit (INTU), Linde (LIN) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM).

Ecolab provides water, hygiene, and infection prevention solutions across industrial, healthcare, and institutional end markets. Its offerings are embedded in customer operations and often linked to regulatory requirements, safety standards, and efficiency objectives. Delivered through a global service network, these solutions contribute to high customer retention and a recurring revenue profile. We believe the company's scale, technical capabilities, and long-standing customer relationships support a durable competitive position.

The company's model combines consumable products with service and monitoring, which contributes to revenue visibility and resilience. Because its solutions are integral to maintaining uptime and compliance, demand tends to be less discretionary. Ecolab has also expanded its capabilities through digital tools and analytics, which we believe can enhance customer outcomes and deepen integration within client operations. These factors may support pricing and reinforce its role as a value-added partner.

Ecolab continues to invest in areas such as water management, energy efficiency, and sustainability, which we believe extend its growth opportunities while leveraging its existing platform. The company has demonstrated a history of operational discipline and capital allocation, contributing to attractive returns on invested capital. While macro and execution risks remain, we believe long-term demand drivers, including water scarcity and regulatory requirements, support the durability of the business model and may contribute to steady growth and margin improvement over time.

GE Vernova is a global leader in the electric power industry, providing products and services across the electricity value chain. Following its April 2024 spin-off from General Electric (GE), GE Vernova operates as an independent company focused on power, wind and electrification. GEV's installed base helps generate approximately 25% of the world's electricity. The company maintains one of the largest installed fleets of heavy-duty gas turbines globally. GEV's combination of equipment sales and high-margin service revenue creates a robust backlog and long-term earnings visibility. We believe GEV possesses a durable competitive moat driven by its scale, technological leadership and deep relationships with governments, utilities and industrial customers.

Artificial intelligence and data center growth are key demand drivers. The projected energy required to enable this growth is significant, with the International Energy Agency expecting global data center electricity consumption to double by 2030 and triple by 2035 compared to 2024. This dynamic is driving significant investment in reliable power generation and grid modernization, areas where GEV is well positioned to benefit.

Although power equipment markets are cyclical and capital intensive, we believe these characteristics favor scaled incumbents. The magnitude of required investment, long project cycles and technical complexity create high barriers to entry. As legacy offshore wind projects roll off in 2027 and global energy demand rises, we see an opportunity for margin expansion driven by improved execution and operating leverage. We believe GEV is well-positioned to continue generating strong returns on invested capital and remain a critical player in the global electricity value chain.

QXO Incorporated is a building products distribution platform pursuing a consolidation strategy within a highly fragmented, ~$200 billion addressable market. The industry remains predominantly local and operationally fragmented, with many subscale distributors lacking purchasing power, logistics scale, and technological capabilities. QXO's strategy is to aggregate these assets into a national platform through acquisitions and operational standardization. We believe the size of the market and the company's acquisition pipeline provide a meaningful opportunity to build a scaled operator over time.

In parallel, the industry is undergoing a gradual shift toward more digital and data-driven operations. As QXO invests in systems, data analytics, and pricing tools, we believe there is potential to improve pricing consistency, inventory management, and service levels. These capabilities may enhance efficiency while strengthening customer relationships, particularly as workflows become more integrated. In an environment where reliability and product availability are critical, scaled operators may be better positioned to compete with smaller peers.

As the platform expands, we believe these operational and data advantages could become more pronounced. The investment case is further supported by a capital allocation strategy led by Brad Jacobs, focused on acquiring subscale businesses and seeking to improve performance through procurement, logistics, and technology initiatives. While execution risk is inherent in any consolidation strategy, particularly at scale, we believe QXO operates in a target-rich environment with a potential runway for growth. Over time, we believe the combination of scale, operational improvement, and end-market demand may support revenue growth, margin expansion, and attractive returns on invested capital.

We sold our remaining stub position in Salesforce.com, as the range of potential outcomes has widened due to the growing risk that AI will disrupt the traditional seat-based software revenue model, despite Salesforce's efforts to aggressively build an agentic enterprise platform. We believe the shift from a seat-based to a consumption-based model may limit long-term revenue growth, as seat-based attrition is likely to partially cannibalize the incremental revenue opportunity from consumption-based pricing. In addition, the consumption model is likely to carry a higher cost of goods sold, which could pressure margins over time. Given the uncertainty around the company's future revenue and margin profile during this transition, we chose to exit the remaining position.

We also sold our remaining position in SAP ADR during the quarter, as the range of outcomes expanded amid growing perceived risk of disruption to traditional software from AI. Solutions enabled by large language models, such as Anthropic's Claude Code, are introducing new economic models that challenge the durability of software-as-a-service (SaaS) incumbents like SAP.

We believe that as enterprises continue to adopt generative AI solutions, including agentic computing, traditional SaaS revenue models will shift from a per-seat pricing structure to a consumption-based model. This transition is likely to introduce greater volatility in out-year revenue

streams, potentially pressure gross margins, and shift the user interface from the application layer (controlled by SaaS providers) to the agentic layer, which may be supported by third-party vendors, including large language model providers. Given these dynamics, we exited our remaining stub position and redeployed the capital into opportunities with a more attractive risk-reward profile.

Veralto Corporation reported a modest fourth quarter earnings beat and a slight increase to guidance. Nevertheless, slowing longer-term revenue trends and M&A activity perceived as incremental were insufficient to justify additional upside at the prevailing valuation. Although we continue to regard Veralto as a high-quality business with strong competitive positions across its Water Quality and Product Identification segments, we exited the position during the quarter and reallocated capital into an opportunity we believe offers a more compelling risk/reward opportunity and stronger long-term growth prospects.

The secondary benchmark is the S&P 500® Index, defined as an unmanaged, capitalization weighted index of the common stocks of 500 major U.S. corporations. In addition, unlike the composite, which periodically maintains a significant cash position, the S&P 500® Index is fully invested. Investors cannot directly invest in an index.

The dispersion in gross-of-fees composite returns shown herein was measured using an asset-weighted standard deviation formula. Performance results presented reflect the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Gross performance is net of all transaction costs, and as of 1/1/25, net returns for all periods are calculated using a model fee, by deducting 1/12th of 0.75% from monthly gross returns.
